channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scientists identify the origin of noise in spin qubit quantum processors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a crucial step toward practical quantum computers by pinpointing the sources of decoherence that plague spin qubit systems, which are among the most promising candidates for large-scale quantum processors due to their compatibility with existing semiconductor fabrication. Understanding and controlling noise is absolutely essential for quantum error correction to work effectively, as even the most sophisticated error correction codes can only handle errors below certain thresholds. The fact that spin qubits are now achieving fidelities that exceed requirements for surface code implementation is particularly exciting, as this puts them in serious contention with other leading qubit technologies like superconducting circuits. By identifying specific noise mechanisms, researchers can now engineer targeted solutions to push spin qubit performance even further, potentially accelerating the timeline for fault-tolerant quantum computing.

A spin qubit, in which quantum information is encoded in the spin state of an electron, is one of the most promising platforms for quantum computing. Spin qubits exhibit long coherence times and are compatible with advanced semiconductor manufacturing technologies. The leading implementation of spin qubits involves confined electrons inside quantum dots, a nanoscale semiconductor architecture that behaves like a controllable artificial atom. Recent advances have enabled high-fidelity operation of single- and two-qubit gates, exceeding the threshold required for certain surface code quantum error correction techniques.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: SEALSQ Broadens Quantum Computing and Post-Quantum Security Strategy
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement signals a notable shift in how cybersecurity companies are positioning themselves for the quantum era, with SEALSQ expanding beyond traditional post-quantum cryptography into broader quantum computing investments. The move reflects growing industry recognition that quantum-safe security will require deep integration across the entire quantum technology stack, from hardware to software to implementation protocols. While many companies focus solely on developing quantum-resistant algorithms, SEALSQ's multi-pronged approach through acquisitions could provide valuable insights into how quantum and post-quantum technologies will converge in practical deployments. This strategy represents an interesting bet that success in quantum security will require understanding and controlling multiple layers of the quantum computing ecosystem rather than just the cryptographic endpoints.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — SEALSQ Corp (NASDAQ: LAES) (“SEALSQ” or “Company”), a company that focuses on developing and selling Semiconductors, PKI, and Post-Quantum technology hardware and software products, today updated on its strategic expansion into the full quantum technology stack through targeted investments and acquisitions across leading quantum computing companies. Building on its established […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Companies Building Quantum Computing Chips in 2026
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article captures a fascinating moment in quantum computing where the field has matured beyond academic research labs but hasn't yet converged on a single dominant technology platform, much like the early days of personal computing when dozens of incompatible architectures competed for market share. The diversity of approaches being pursued commercially in 2026 reflects both the enormous technical challenges of building fault-tolerant quantum computers and the genuine uncertainty about which physical implementation will ultimately prove most practical for real-world applications. Each modality brings unique advantages and trade-offs, from the fast gate operations of superconducting qubits to the long coherence times of trapped ions, creating a rich competitive landscape that should accelerate innovation across all platforms. This technological pluralism is actually healthy for the field, as it ensures multiple pathways toward the holy grail of fault-tolerant quantum computing rather than betting everything on a single approach.

Insider Brief Quantum computing hardware in 2026 looks like several distinct technologies, each with different physics, different manufacturing constraints, and different ideas about what fault-tolerant quantum computing should look like when it arrives. Superconducting qubits, trapped ions, photonics, neutral atoms, and silicon spin qubits are all in active commercial development. No modality has established the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scientists Identify the Origin of Noise in Spin Qubit Quantum Processors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a crucial step toward building more reliable quantum computers by pinpointing the specific sources of noise that plague spin qubit systems. While spin qubits are among the most promising quantum computing platforms due to their compatibility with existing semiconductor manufacturing and relatively long coherence times, noise has remained a persistent challenge limiting their performance. By identifying exactly where this noise originates, scientists can now develop targeted mitigation strategies rather than taking a scattershot approach to improving qubit quality. This kind of fundamental understanding is essential for scaling spin qubit systems from laboratory demonstrations to the large-scale, error-corrected quantum computers needed for practical applications.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — A spin qubit, in which quantum information is encoded in the spin state of an electron, is one of the most promising platforms for quantum computing. Spin qubits exhibit long coherence times and are compatible with advanced semiconductor manufacturing technologies. The leading implementation of spin qubits involves confined electrons inside […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Chinese Scientists Demonstrate Quantum Random Access Memory Architecture Aimed at Solving Data Bottleneck
item.description:
Curator's Take: This demonstration of quantum random access memory (QRAM) on a superconducting processor represents a crucial step toward making quantum computers practically useful for real-world applications that involve large classical datasets. The data bottleneck has long been one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges - while quantum processors excel at certain computations, they've struggled with efficiently accessing and manipulating the vast amounts of classical data that many algorithms require. By showing that QRAM can work on actual quantum hardware rather than just in theory, the Chinese team has opened a pathway for quantum computers to tackle problems in machine learning, optimization, and database search that were previously impractical due to data access limitations. This breakthrough could significantly accelerate the timeline for quantum advantage in commercially relevant applications that blend quantum processing with classical data.

Insider Brief Chinese researchers have experimentally demonstrated a quantum random access memory architecture, or QRAM, on a superconducting quantum processor, marking what researchers describe as a significant step toward improving how quantum computers access large amounts of conventional data. The work, published recently in Nature Physics by a team led by Zhejiang University and highlighted […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Classiq and UC Chile Launch Quantum Computing Research for Biomedical Imaging
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration between Classiq and UC Chile represents a promising convergence of quantum computing with practical healthcare applications, specifically targeting the computationally intensive challenge of medical image analysis where quantum algorithms could potentially offer significant advantages. The project's hybrid approach, combining quantum algorithms with classical machine learning on NVIDIA's CUDA-Q platform, exemplifies the current industry trend toward near-term quantum-classical systems that can deliver practical benefits before fault-tolerant quantum computers arrive. Biomedical imaging is particularly well-suited for quantum enhancement given the complex pattern recognition and optimization problems involved in pathology analysis, making this a strategic research direction that could accelerate both quantum algorithm development and medical diagnostics capabilities.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Classiq and Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile (UC Chile) today announced a joint research project to develop hybrid quantum algorithms for biomedical image analysis, assisted by classical machine learning and the NVIDIA CUDA-Q platform for quantum-classical computing. The 12-month engagement, titled “Enhancing Pathology through Quantum Computing,” is funded through Avanza UC 2025, the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantinuum and Mitsubishi Electric Sign Quantum Computing MOU
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership between Quantinuum and Mitsubishi Electric represents a significant step toward bringing quantum computing into real-world industrial applications, particularly in manufacturing and electrical systems where Mitsubishi Electric has decades of expertise. The collaboration could accelerate the development of quantum solutions for complex optimization problems that plague modern manufacturing, from supply chain logistics to energy management systems. What makes this particularly noteworthy is Mitsubishi Electric's practical engineering focus, which could help bridge the gap between quantum computing's theoretical promise and actual deployment in industrial settings. This type of industry-academia partnership is becoming increasingly crucial as quantum computing moves from research labs toward commercial viability.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantinuum, a leading quantum computing company, announced today that it has signed a non-binding Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with Mitsubishi Electric Corporation, a recognized global leader in the manufacturing, marketing, and sale of electrical and electronic equipment and systems, to establish a framework for a strategic partnership aiming at accelerating […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: OQC, JPMorganChase and AMD Commence Research Collaboration to Develop New Quantum-AI Platform in London
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration represents a significant step toward practical quantum-AI integration, with JPMorganChase becoming one of the first major financial institutions to test quantum algorithms in a dedicated enterprise-grade environment rather than relying solely on cloud access. The partnership combines OQC's quantum hardware expertise with AMD's classical computing infrastructure, creating a hybrid platform that could accelerate the development of quantum advantage applications in financial modeling and risk analysis. What makes this particularly noteworthy is the focus on building secure, on-premises quantum infrastructure in London, potentially establishing a template for how other enterprises might deploy quantum computing capabilities within their own data centers rather than depending entirely on external quantum cloud services.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — OQC, JPMorganChase and AMD today announced a research collaboration leveraging a new and dedicated Quantum-AI Data Centre, built by OQC in London. JPMorganChase researchers will test near-term quantum and hybrid quantum-classical computing applications via a secure enterprise environment to examine how quantum computing, AI and high-performance classical infrastructure can work […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: C12 Automates Pick & Place Nanoassembly to Standardize Carbon Nanotube Qubit Fabrication
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development marks a crucial step toward making carbon nanotube qubits manufacturable at scale, addressing one of the biggest challenges facing this promising quantum computing platform. C12's Pick & Place process cleverly separates the high-temperature nanotube growth from the delicate quantum circuit fabrication, potentially solving the thermal incompatibility issues that have plagued CNT qubit production. Carbon nanotubes offer unique advantages like naturally long coherence times and single-atom precision, but until now have been notoriously difficult to integrate into reliable manufacturing workflows. This automation breakthrough could position carbon nanotube qubits as serious competitors to superconducting and trapped-ion systems by making them economically viable for quantum processors.

Quantum hardware venture C12 has introduced Pick & Place, a patented nanoassembly process engineered to transfer single-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) onto pre-fabricated quantum circuits with micrometric precision. The fabrication method serves as a foundational manufacturing block to decouple high-temperature nanotube growth from sensitive, sub-micron chip lithography layers. By adapting advanced semiconductor packaging concepts to the [...] The post C12 Automates Pick & Place Nanoassembly to Standardize Carbon Nanotube Qubit Fabrication appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantinuum and Mitsubishi Electric Sign Agreement to Integrate Trapped-Ion Hardware into Industrial Design Lifecycles
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership represents a significant step toward quantum computing's integration into real-world industrial applications, moving beyond academic research toward practical engineering workflows. By combining Quantinuum's high-fidelity trapped-ion systems with Mitsubishi Electric's established industrial simulation expertise, the collaboration could accelerate quantum advantage in complex manufacturing optimization problems where classical computers struggle with exponential scaling. The focus on integrating quantum hardware directly into existing design lifecycles is particularly noteworthy, as it addresses the critical challenge of making quantum computing accessible to engineers who aren't quantum specialists. While still early-stage with the non-binding MOU, this type of industry-quantum hardware partnership could serve as a template for bringing quantum benefits to traditional engineering sectors.

Quantinuum and Mitsubishi Electric Corporation have signed a non-binding Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) to build a joint development framework for advanced industrial engineering and manufacturing design. The partnership aims to combine Quantinuum’s high-fidelity trapped-ion hardware platforms with Mitsubishi Electric’s extensive industrial simulation libraries. The strategic alignment is structured to transition computer-aided engineering workflows from purely [...] The post Quantinuum and Mitsubishi Electric Sign Agreement to Integrate Trapped-Ion Hardware into Industrial Design Lifecycles appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantinuum Prices Upsized Initial Public Offering at $1.68 Billion to Landmark Public Market Debut
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article marks a watershed moment for the quantum computing industry as Quantinuum becomes one of the first major quantum hardware companies to go public, raising $1.68 billion in what represents the largest quantum computing IPO to date. The successful upsizing of their offering signals strong investor confidence in trapped-ion quantum computing technology and validates the commercial potential of quantum systems beyond just research applications. Quantinuum's public debut provides crucial capital for scaling their quantum hardware and software platforms while establishing a benchmark valuation that will likely influence funding decisions across the entire quantum ecosystem. This IPO represents a maturation milestone for quantum computing, transitioning from a purely venture-backed sector to attracting mainstream public market investment.

Quantinuum Inc. has finalized the pricing of its upsized initial public offering (IPO) on the Nasdaq Global Market under the ticker symbol QNT. The transaction structure issued 28,000,000 shares of Class A common stock at a public offering price of $60.00 per share, expanding the initial capital targets to accumulate approximately $1.68 billion in gross proceeds. Evaluated at the final pricing metrics, [...] The post Quantinuum Prices Upsized Initial Public Offering at $1.68 Billion to Landmark Public Market Debut appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Breakeven demonstration of quantum low-density parity-check codes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research marks a crucial milestone in quantum error correction by demonstrating breakeven performance with quantum low-density parity-check codes, where logical qubits actually live as long as the underlying physical qubits - a key threshold for practical quantum computing. The team's achievement of encoding 4 logical qubits into just 18 physical qubits while maintaining error rates 9 times better than previous demonstrations shows the promise of qLDPC codes as more efficient alternatives to the widely-studied surface code. What makes this particularly impressive is the use of trapped-ion hardware's inherent flexibility to test nine different error correction schemes on the same device, proving these codes can work without exotic hardware modifications like long-range couplers that other platforms might require. The novel OMG architecture implementation that eliminates the need for ion shuttling could significantly streamline future trapped-ion quantum computers while making sophisticated error correction more practical.

High-rate quantum low-density parity-check (qLDPC) codes are a leading candidate for fault-tolerant quantum computing. They feature higher encoding rates than planar alternatives such as the surface code, but their implementation often entails significant hardware hurdles like the need for long-range couplers. We leverage the flexibility of a trapped-ion quantum computer to demonstrate nine quantum error-correcting codes with starkly different qubit connectivity requirements on a single device without any hardware reconfiguration. These experiments span three families of quantum error-correcting codes: qLDPC codes, topological codes, and concatenated codes. With a qLDPC code encoding 4 logical qubits into 18 physical qubits, we achieve a logical error rate up to $9\times$ better than a previous demonstration of a similar code on superconducting solid-state qubits. Moreover, our implementation exhibits breakeven performance, with some instances achieving qubit lifetimes comparable to or slightly exceeding that of our trapped-ion qubits. We use a novel implementation of the optical-metastable-ground (OMG) architecture for addressable mid-circuit measurement and reset, which enables us to perform these experiments without any ion transport or dedicated coolant ions, requirements that typically consume a large fraction of the runtime or ion count of trapped-ion quantum computers.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Nanostructure modelling with early fault tolerant quantum computers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research presents a compelling chicken-and-egg scenario where near-term fault-tolerant quantum computers could accelerate the development of better quantum hardware by simulating the complex multi-electron dynamics in quantum dots that are too difficult for classical computers to handle accurately. The authors provide realistic resource estimates showing that meaningful simulations of 4-8 electron systems could run on quantum computers with hundreds of thousands of physical qubits within reasonable timeframes, making this a prime candidate for early quantum advantage applications. What makes this particularly exciting is that improved understanding of quantum dot behavior could directly feed back into better spin-qubit designs and quantum sensing devices, creating a virtuous cycle where quantum computers help design their own next generation. The practical focus on semiconductor nanostructures also bridges quantum computing with established semiconductor manufacturing, potentially accelerating commercial quantum technology development.

Semiconductor nanostructures are central to many developing technologies. Notably, double quantum dots are especially important for semiconductor spin-qubit architectures, quantum sensing applications, and quantum-dot solar cells. Accurate modelling is highly desirable but conventional methods can struggle when dynamics involve more than two interacting electrons. In this work, we present a quantum simulation framework capable of addressing multi-electron double quantum dots. We adopt an efficiently scaling 1$^\text{st}$ quantised representation of the system and develop algorithms based on both Trotterisation and qubitisation. Incorporating insights from classical simulations enables us to produce resource estimates that are more realistic than those obtained from theoretical error bounds. Using a standard surface code model with physical noise at $10^{-3}$, our results indicate that the ground-state energy of four electrons in a double quantum dot can be estimated in approximately 24 hours using 226k physical qubits, or an eight-electron system in 3.4 days with 314k qubits (with runtimes falling dramatically when more qubits are available). We anticipate that incorporating very recent advances including dense surface code architectures (Low et al. arXiv:2605.30455) may reduce these costs significantly further. We conclude that early fault tolerant computers may prove to be valuable tools for designing mature-era quantum technologies.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Serendipity Capital Leads $5 Million Seed Financing Round for Next-Gen Optical Atomic Clock Developer QuantX Labs
item.description:
Curator's Take: This funding round highlights the growing commercial viability of quantum sensing applications, particularly precision timekeeping, which often serves as a stepping stone for companies before tackling full quantum computing systems. QuantX Labs' focus on optical atomic clocks addresses critical infrastructure needs for telecommunications, GPS systems, and financial trading networks, where ultra-precise timing can translate directly into competitive advantages and revenue. The $5 million investment from Serendipity Capital signals institutional confidence that quantum sensing technologies are approaching market readiness, potentially offering investors more near-term returns compared to the longer development timelines typically associated with fault-tolerant quantum computers. This represents part of a broader trend where quantum sensing and metrology companies are securing significant funding as they bridge the gap between laboratory demonstrations and practical commercial applications.

Permanent capital firm Serendipity Capital has led the seed fundraising round for QuantX Labs, an Adelaide-based company specializing in precision quantum sensing and timekeeping. Serendipity Capital allocated US$5 million ($A7 million) into the equity tranche, marking the first external institutional financing secured by the hardware developer. The capital deployment is designated to scale manufacturing lines, [...] The post Serendipity Capital Leads $5 Million Seed Financing Round for Next-Gen Optical Atomic Clock Developer QuantX Labs appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Semidefinite-programming hierarchies for classically simulable state families
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's most fundamental questions: when do quantum states actually provide an advantage over classical systems, and when are they just classical phenomena in disguise? The researchers developed a systematic mathematical framework using semidefinite programming to determine whether families of quantum states can be simulated classically, providing both computational tools to test for genuine quantum advantage and methods to find the optimal classical simulations when they exist. This is particularly valuable for understanding the boundary between quantum and classical resources in noisy quantum devices, where decoherence can wash out quantum advantages and leave systems that appear quantum but are actually classically simulable. The framework could help quantum algorithm designers identify when their protocols retain genuine quantum benefits despite noise, and guide experimentalists in determining the noise thresholds beyond which their quantum devices lose their computational edge.

Identifying whether a state family admits an irreducible quantum advantage is a fundamental task in quantum resource theory and quantum information processing. Here we study classically simulable state families, namely those residing within the convex hull of pairwise commuting families and therefore admitting a classical explanation. We develop a complete semidefinite-programming (SDP) hierarchy characterizing the set of classically simulable state families in arbitrary finite dimension. The key step is to reformulate classical simulability as a feasibility problem over deterministic response functions and auxiliary positive-operator-valued measures (POVMs) simulable by rank-one projective measurements. We establish a complete SDP hierarchy for rank-one projectively simulable POVMs and transfer the resulting characterization to state families, yielding both primal feasibility tests and dual affine witnesses certifying failure of classical simulability. Applying the hierarchy to state families mixed with depolarizing noise gives computable upper bounds on the critical classical visibility, which are matched by explicit classical simulations in several symmetric examples. These results provide a systematic convex-optimization framework for certifying classical simulability of quantum state families.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UK Risks Repeating Semiconductor and AI Mistakes Unless Government Acts Now on Quantum, Warns Cross-Party Parliamentary Group
item.description:
Curator's Take: This parliamentary warning highlights a critical inflection point for the UK's quantum ambitions, as the country that pioneered national quantum investment now faces the risk of losing its early-mover advantage without sustained government commitment. The cross-party concern echoes painful lessons from semiconductors and AI, where initial UK leadership gave way to dominance by other nations that maintained stronger, more coordinated public-private partnerships. With quantum technologies approaching commercial viability across computing, sensing, and communications, the timing of this alert couldn't be more crucial - the next few years will likely determine which countries capture the lion's share of what analysts project to be a massive quantum economy. The UK's decade-long head start and £1 billion investment could prove meaningless if competitors like the US, China, and EU continue ramping up their quantum spending while British support wavers.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The United Kingdom launched the world’s first National Quantum Technologies Programme in 2014, and has invested more than £1 billion over the past decade in building an ecosystem that spans quantum computing, sensing, communications and timing. According to analysis cited in the paper, quantum computing alone is projected to create […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Deployed trusted-node quantum key distribution over 300 km with a multi-core fiber access link
item.description:
Curator's Take: This demonstration represents a significant step toward practical quantum key distribution deployment by successfully operating QKD systems over Sweden's actual telecommunications infrastructure spanning 303 kilometers, including multi-core fiber segments that will be crucial for future quantum-classical hybrid networks. The researchers tackled real-world challenges by showing QKD can coexist with classical Ethernet traffic, handle dynamic channel switching between fiber cores, and operate through the high losses encountered in deployed fiber networks using advanced superconducting detectors. Perhaps most importantly, the team went beyond just proving the technology works to examine how limited QKD key generation rates affect actual encrypted communications, revealing that image transmission quality depends heavily on available key budgets and compression choices. This kind of end-to-end analysis of QKD performance in realistic network conditions provides invaluable insights for organizations planning quantum-secured communications infrastructure.

Quantum key distribution (QKD) is increasingly considered for deployment in realistic communication networks, where long distances, heterogeneous fiber infrastructure, and coexistence with classical traffic present substantial challenges. Here, we demonstrate trusted-node QKD between Linköping University and the Stockholm hub of the Swedish national quantum communication infrastructure over 270 km of deployed single-mode fiber, extended by a 33 km multi-core fiber (MCF) segment emulating a metropolitan access link, for a total distance of 303 km. The two sub-links use commercial QKD systems whose receivers are interfaced with external superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors, enabling operation at losses beyond those supported by standard internal gated-mode detectors. We operate the link while actively switching the QKD channel between two MCF cores, with co-propagating Ethernet traffic and injected broadband optical noise in the other cores. The results demonstrate the integration of commercial QKD into demanding, dynamically reconfigurable fiber infrastructure relevant to future hybrid quantum-classical networks. Finally, using the generated secret keys, we illustrate how limited and time-varying QKD throughput affects one-time-pad-protected image transmission: image fidelity depends strongly on the available QKD-generated key budget and the choice of compression algorithm, highlighting application-level challenges for QKD-based encryption in realistic scenarios.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Download: AI-generated lawsuits and virtual power plants for data centers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article appears to be a general technology newsletter digest rather than focused quantum computing content, covering AI-generated lawsuits and data center power infrastructure. While these topics intersect with the broader tech ecosystem that quantum computing operates within, this particular piece doesn't contain quantum-specific developments or insights that would be directly relevant to quantum computing audiences. The mention of data center power infrastructure could tangentially relate to quantum computing's energy requirements, but without quantum-specific context, this article falls outside the core focus areas that quantum computing enthusiasts would find most valuable.

This is today’s edition of The Download, our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what’s going on in the world of technology. How courts are coping with a flood of AI-generated lawsuits Most days in her chambers, Judge Maritza Braswell, a federal magistrate judge in Colorado, sifts through stacks of documents written by…

Read original at MIT Technology Review
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum X Labs Adds Oren Raz to Scientific Advisory Board
item.description:
Curator's Take: This appointment signals Quantum X Labs' strategic push to strengthen its scientific foundations by bringing aboard one of Israel's leading theoretical physicists, particularly notable given the Weizmann Institute's stellar reputation in quantum research. Prof. Raz's expertise in statistical mechanics and non-equilibrium physics could prove valuable as the company navigates the complex theoretical challenges inherent in scaling quantum technologies. While advisory board additions are common in the quantum space, securing talent from Israel's premier research institutions suggests Quantum X Labs is serious about bridging the gap between cutting-edge academic research and commercial quantum applications. The timing appears strategic as the company likely prepares for its next phase of development in an increasingly competitive quantum market.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantum X Labs Inc. (Nasdaq: QXL), an advanced quantum technologies company, today announces the appointment of Prof. Oren Raz of the Weizmann Institute of Science to its Scientific Advisory Board. Prof. Raz’s appointment brings one of Israel’s most distinguished physicists into the company’s scientific leadership at a pivotal moment in […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Barbell Codes: qLDPC Codes for Superconducting Quantum Hardware
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most pressing challenges in quantum error correction by introducing "barbell" qLDPC codes that can be efficiently implemented on real superconducting quantum hardware without requiring exotic connectivity patterns. The breakthrough lies in designing both the error correction code and the chip layout together, ensuring that all necessary qubit interactions can be performed natively on a 2D grid while maintaining constant hardware complexity as the system scales up. With simulations showing these codes can protect quantum information for trillions of error correction cycles using fewer than 30 physical qubits per logical qubit at realistic noise levels, this work provides a concrete pathway toward fault-tolerant quantum computing on near-term hardware. The practical focus on implementability rather than just theoretical performance marks a crucial step from laboratory concepts toward deployable quantum error correction systems.

The major challenge on the way to fault-tolerant quantum computing comes from the insufficient quality of hardware components and the difficulty of scaling their number without further compromising fidelity. Quantum Low-Density Parity-Check (qLDPC) codes offer a promising solution by encoding logical qubits with low overhead and at a comparatively high code distance. However, it remains an open question how to scalably implement efficient qLDPC codes on fixed-connectivity quantum chips without increasing hardware complexity to enable the non-local interactions in their underlying QEC cycles. We resolve this challenge for the first time by introducing a family of qLDPC "barbell" codes accompanied by a realistic chip layout that natively supports all required two-qubit interactions. Crucially, the hardware complexity required to implement barbell codes remains constant as code distance increases. We provide a detailed investigation into the feasibility of all required hardware components and simulate a specific family of barbell codes against circuit-level noise. We find that, with a modest overhead of $<30$ data qubits per logical qubit, barbell codes can preserve information at a physical noise strength of $10^{-4}$ for several trillion QEC cycles. Simulations of logical multi-Pauli measurements, performed with circuits tailored to the chip, yield similar logical performance per QEC round, indicating that entangling gates between logical qubits in barbell codes can be realized fault-tolerantly.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computing for accurate large-scale electronic-structure calculations: DFT-embedded, post-processed quantum-selected configuration interaction
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a major step toward making quantum computers practically useful for chemistry by cleverly combining quantum and classical methods in a layered approach. The team's multilevel embedding framework uses quantum algorithms only where they're most needed - for the trickiest electron correlations - while relying on established classical methods for the bulk of the calculation, maximizing efficiency on today's limited quantum hardware. Achieving 1 kcal/mol accuracy for chemical reactions using just a subset of a 144-qubit system demonstrates that meaningful quantum advantage for real-world chemistry problems may be closer than expected. This hybrid approach could be the key to unlocking quantum computing's potential for drug discovery, materials design, and catalysis long before we have fault-tolerant quantum computers.

We present a multilevel embedding framework for quantum chemistry calculations on a quantum computer. In our framework, a quantum algorithm treats the strongly correlated active space, while a high-level wave-function method such as coupled cluster theory or multireference perturbation theory recovers the remaining correlation in the surrounding region. A sampling-based quantum algorithm, quantum-selected configuration interaction, bridges the quantum and classical treatments. The entire calculation is embedded in a low-cost density functional theory description of the surrounding environment using Manby's projection technique. We apply the framework to organic, metal-organic, and metallic systems, computing bond dissociation energies, adsorption energies, and reaction barriers using only the subset of qubits of a 144-qubit superconducting quantum computer at the University of Osaka and achieving $\sim$1 kcal/mol agreement with classical references for a Menshutkin $\mathrm{S_N2}$ reaction inside a carbon nanotube. Our results may open the way to quantitatively reliable quantum-classical hybrid calculations for large-scale chemical systems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Rapid Gaussian Boson Sampling Circuit Screening for GKP States Creation via a Two-Stage Machine Learning Surrogate
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges: creating the exotic quantum states needed for fault-tolerant computation without breaking the bank computationally. GKP states are like the Swiss Army knife of photonic quantum computing - they can encode logical qubits while naturally protecting against certain errors - but generating them through Gaussian Boson Sampling requires calculating mathematical functions so complex they're in the "#P-complete" category of computational nightmares. The researchers' clever solution uses machine learning as a fast screening tool to identify promising quantum circuits before running the expensive exact calculations, achieving 90% accuracy while dramatically reducing computational overhead. This approach could accelerate the development of practical photonic quantum computers by making it feasible to explore the vast space of possible quantum circuits for generating these crucial resource states.

Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) states are essential non-Gaussian resources for fault-tolerant photonic quantum computing, enabling logical qubit encoding with intrinsic robustness against errors. Several approaches to GKP state preparation have been explored, including measurement-based protocols in circuit QED and trapped-ion systems, cat-state breeding, and photon-subtraction schemes. However, these methods are either restricted to specific platforms or require deep non-Gaussian resource chains with exponentially low success probabilities. Gaussian Boson Sampling (GBS) offers a compelling all-photonic alternative by generating non-Gaussian states through measurement-induced nonlinearity, without the need for matter-based ancilla or active feedforward. Nevertheless, its practical implementation is limited by the exponential computational cost of evaluating matrix hafnians-#P-complete functions that govern photon-number probabilities. To address this challenge, we introduce a two-stage Histogram Gradient Boosting surrogate pipeline that predicts, without any hafnian computation, the optimal heralding pattern, circuit fidelity, and post-selection probability for candidate GBS circuits, while reserving exact quantum simulation exclusively for surrogate-selected candidates. Trained on circuit configurations across 3-5 optical modes, the surrogate achieves 90.0% GKP-detection accuracy on a held-out set, representing a 23.7 percentage-point improvement over the baseline, with a fidelity mean absolute error of 0.032 and a log-scale post-selection probability $R^2 = 0.837$, reducing the total simulation burden by approximately 90%.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Shattering the Symmetry Trap in Fixed-Ansatz VQE: An Accelerated ADAPT-VQE Study of Three Pillar Molecules under Bravyi-Kitaev Mapping
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a critical roadblock in quantum chemistry simulations where traditional VQE algorithms get stuck in "symmetry traps" that prevent them from finding correct molecular ground states, particularly when using the Bravyi-Kitaev qubit mapping scheme. The researchers demonstrate that ADAPT-VQE can dynamically break through these limitations by adaptively selecting the most important quantum operators, achieving exact solutions for key test molecules like water and lithium hydride where fixed approaches completely fail. What makes this especially significant is their development of an accelerated computational engine that bypasses the typical scaling bottlenecks, potentially making these more sophisticated VQE variants practical for near-term quantum hardware. This work represents an important step toward making quantum computers genuinely useful for molecular simulation by solving fundamental algorithmic challenges that have limited previous approaches.

Fixed-ansatz Variational Quantum Eigensolvers (VQE), such as the Unitary Coupled Cluster with Singles and Doubles (UCCSD) framework, frequently suffer from severe initialization paralyzation and zero-gradient traps when evaluated using the non-local Bravyi-Kitaev (BK) fermion-to-qubit mapping. In this work, we systematically demonstrate how the Adaptive Derivative-Assembled Pseudo-Trotter (ADAPT-VQE) framework shatters these structural limitations across three distinct electronic and geometric molecular pillars: Lithium Hydride ($\text{LiH}$), Hydrogen Fluoride ($\text{HF}$), and Water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$), under heavily stretched or asymmetric multi-reference configurations. While conventional UCCSD-VQE flatlines completely at a zero energy shift ($0.000000$~Ha) due to global phase cancellations inherent to the BK tree structures, our dynamic ADAPT-VQE loop successfully isolates the dominant symmetry-breaking operators using analytical commutator gradients. To bypass the severe $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ computational bottlenecks of dense matrix exponentiation and Singular Value Decomposition on larger registers, we implement a highly optimized, vector-based Taylor series expansion state-evolution engine. Our numerical results show that the accelerated ADAPT-VQE framework achieves instant, exact Full Configuration Interaction (FCI) convergence within the very first macro-cycle across all three molecular systems, maintaining absolute numerical stability up to a 12-qubit register space. This study establishes a robust, hardware-efficient path for simulating strongly correlated, highly polarized triatomic chemical environments on near-term local architectures.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: C12 Introduces Nanoassembly Process for Carbon Nanotube Quantum Chips
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a significant manufacturing breakthrough that could reshape how quantum processors are built, as C12's Pick & Place nanoassembly technology addresses one of the most challenging aspects of carbon nanotube quantum computing: precisely positioning individual nanotubes on silicon chips. Carbon nanotube qubits offer compelling advantages including long coherence times and compatibility with existing semiconductor manufacturing, but the field has been held back by the difficulty of reliably placing these nanoscale structures exactly where they need to be. C12's proprietary approach could potentially unlock scalable manufacturing of carbon nanotube quantum processors, offering an alternative pathway to the silicon and superconducting qubit platforms that currently dominate the industry. This development represents the kind of foundational engineering advance that often precedes major leaps in quantum computing capability and commercialization.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — C12, a Paris-based quantum computing company developing carbon nanotube spin qubit processors, today announced its proprietary Pick & Place nanoassembly technology: a custom-built, patented nanoassembly method developed entirely in-house to transfer individual carbon nanotubes onto silicon chips with micrometric precision, in controlled vacuum or inert atmosphere conditions. Two setups are […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: No-go theorems on simulating uncertainty principle's signatures
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work provides crucial theoretical foundations for understanding when quantum advantages are truly "quantum" by proving that strong signatures of the uncertainty principle cannot be faked with classical single measurements, even with quantum preprocessing. The research addresses a fundamental question in quantum information science: whether the advantages we attribute to quantum uncertainty might actually be achievable through simpler, less resource-intensive methods. By establishing these "no-go theorems," the authors help validate that certain quantum sensing and communication protocols genuinely require the unique properties of quantum mechanics, rather than just clever classical tricks. This kind of theoretical grounding is essential for the field as it moves toward practical applications, ensuring that claimed quantum advantages represent real computational or sensing benefits worth the engineering complexity of quantum systems.

Uncertainty principle, one of the most iconic features of quantum mechanics, was originally viewed as a fundamental limitation. Since the inception of quantum information science, researchers began to use it to achieve quantum advantages. To better understand the origin of these advantages, an essential question is: To what extent can the uncertainty principle's signatures be simulated by a single measurement? As a single measurement clearly cannot demonstrate the uncertainty principle, such a simulation, if exists, implies the claimed advantages may either stem from other quantum features, or just be reproducible in a less resourceful way. In this work, we report a series of noise-robust no-go theorems, showing that strong enough signatures of uncertainty principle cannot be simulated by a single measurement, even when assisted by quantum pre- or post-processing. This signature is modelled by complementary instruments. We completely characterise complementary instruments by a numerically feasible measure and show that they are necessary and sufficient resources for the advantage in an operational task that aims to unambiguously send classical information.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Symmetry-adapted qubit encoding with complete active space and Bravyi--Kitaev mapping for quantum chemistry on a quantum computer
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most promising applications—molecular simulation—by introducing a clever encoding scheme that dramatically reduces the number of qubits needed without sacrificing accuracy. The SAE-CAS method exploits molecular symmetries to compress quantum chemistry problems into smaller, more manageable forms, potentially making complex molecular simulations feasible on near-term quantum devices that have limited qubit counts. What makes this particularly exciting is that the researchers demonstrate their approach not only uses fewer qubits but also creates shallower quantum circuits that converge faster, addressing multiple bottlenecks that currently limit quantum advantage in chemistry. With an open-source implementation already available, this could accelerate the timeline for quantum computers to tackle real-world drug discovery and materials science problems.

We present a symmetry-adapted qubit encoding with complete active space (SAE-CAS) for quantum chemistry on fault-tolerant and near-term quantum processors. Building on exact-symmetry encodings, we extend symmetry-adapted mappings to approximate $Z$-symmetries corresponding to frozen-core and virtual orbitals, thereby reducing qubit requirements without significant loss of accuracy. We derive the mapping from the second-quantised Hamiltonian to active-space qubit Hamiltonians, prove its equivalence to the canonical CAS Hamiltonian with frozen-core and virtual-orbital projection, and integrate it with point-group and spin-parity symmetry encodings via affine Clifford transformations to maximise qubit reduction while preserving the target symmetry sector. The same framework also accommodates the Bravyi--Kitaev mapping, yielding an SAE-CAS-BK variant that is unitarily equivalent to SAE-CAS. Numerical benchmarking on nine small molecules using UCCSD and a hardware-efficient shifted-circular-alternating (HE-SCA) ansatz shows that SAE-CAS reduces qubit counts and Pauli-operator weight, yields shallower circuits with fewer parameters, and often accelerates VQE convergence; with HE-SCA it consistently reaches CAS reference energies in cases where JW-CAS does not converge within the tested budgets. We provide an open-source implementation in the Python package QuantumSymmetry. SAE-CAS offers a route to resource-efficient molecular simulations on fault-tolerant and near-term quantum processors.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuantX Labs Announces Seed Round With Serendipity Capital Lead Investor
item.description:
Curator's Take: This funding announcement signals growing investor confidence in quantum sensing applications beyond the traditional focus on quantum computing, with QuantX Labs targeting the critical market of positioning, navigation and timing systems. Optical atomic clocks represent one of the most commercially viable near-term quantum technologies, offering precision timing capabilities essential for everything from GPS systems to financial networks where microsecond accuracy matters. The defense and space focus is particularly noteworthy, as these sectors are driving significant demand for quantum-enhanced navigation systems that can operate independently of GPS satellites, addressing both security concerns and the need for robust positioning in contested environments. Serendipity Capital's investment reflects the broader trend of venture funding shifting toward quantum applications with clearer paths to market deployment.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Serendipity Capital, a leading global investor in critical technologies, announced today that it has led the seed fundraising round for QuantX Labs, an Australian company at the forefront of quantum sensing for applications including Positioning, Navigation and Timing. QuantX Labs is developing optical atomic clock technology for defence, space and […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantinuum to Debut on Nasdaq After Raising $1.68 Billion in IPO
item.description:
Curator's Take: This IPO represents a watershed moment for the quantum computing industry, as Quantinuum becomes one of the first major pure-play quantum companies to go public with such substantial investor backing. The $1.68 billion raise at $60 per share signals growing institutional confidence in quantum computing's commercial viability, particularly given Quantinuum's unique position as the merger of Honeywell Quantum Solutions and Cambridge Quantum Computing, combining hardware and software expertise. This public debut will likely serve as a benchmark for how investors value quantum companies and could pave the way for other quantum startups to access public markets. The success or struggles of Quantinuum's public performance will be closely watched as a barometer for the entire quantum computing sector's maturation from research curiosity to investable technology.

Insider Brief Quantinuum is set to begin trading on the Nasdaq today (June 4) after raising $1.68 billion in one of the largest quantum computing initial public offerings to date, a closely watched debut that could shape investor sentiment across the emerging sector. The Broomfield, Colorado-based company upped the price of its shares at $60 […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Yaqumo, NKT Photonics and Hamamatsu Photonics Sign Quantum Photonics Agreement
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership between Yaqumo, NKT Photonics, and Hamamatsu Photonics addresses a crucial but often overlooked bottleneck in quantum computing development: the supply chain for high-performance optical components. While much attention focuses on qubit count and gate fidelities, neutral atom quantum computers depend heavily on sophisticated laser systems and optical devices for atom trapping, manipulation, and readout. This collaboration signals the quantum industry's maturation beyond pure research, as companies recognize that reliable, industrial-grade optical components are essential for scaling quantum systems from laboratory prototypes to commercial products. The move reflects growing awareness that quantum computing success requires not just better algorithms and processors, but also a robust ecosystem of specialized suppliers and manufacturing partners.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — As the industrialization of quantum computing accelerates worldwide, the advancement and stable procurement of optical core components and materials that fundamentally underpin system performance have become critical challenges — beyond the development of quantum processors alone. In cold-atom (neutral atom) quantum computers in particular, high-performance optical devices are essential at […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Design Acquires Quantum Sensing Company Qnami
item.description:
Curator's Take: This acquisition signals a significant consolidation in the quantum sensing market as established instrumentation giant Quantum Design absorbs diamond NV-center specialist Qnami, combining decades of materials characterization expertise with cutting-edge quantum sensing capabilities. The move reflects growing commercial interest in quantum sensing applications, particularly for semiconductor and materials research where diamond-based magnetometry can detect magnetic fields with unprecedented precision at the nanoscale. This merger could accelerate the deployment of quantum sensing tools in industrial settings, bridging the gap between academic research and practical applications in areas like quality control for quantum devices and advanced materials development. The acquisition also demonstrates how traditional scientific instrument companies are positioning themselves in the quantum technology ecosystem by incorporating quantum sensing into their broader portfolio of research tools.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantum Design International, Inc., a leading global provider of scientific instrumentation and advanced materials characterization solutions, announced the acquisition of Qnami, a pioneer in diamond-based quantum sensing technologies. The transaction enhances Quantum Design’s ability to support researchers and industrial customers working at the frontiers of quantum materials, spintronics, nanomagnetism, semiconductors, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Periodic Symmetry-Adapted Encoding: Qubit Reduction in Crystalline Electronic Structure
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough extends symmetry-adapted encoding to crystalline materials, achieving dramatic qubit reductions of 4-8 qubits across diverse crystal structures while preserving chemical accuracy. The work is particularly significant because it exploits unique symmetries available in periodic systems - like half-translation symmetries - that aren't present in molecular calculations, enabling reductions that exceed what's possible with molecules alone. With CNOT gate count reductions up to 309× and variational parameter reductions of 3-8×, this approach could make quantum simulation of industrially relevant materials like semiconductors and ceramics much more feasible on near-term quantum devices. The framework's success across cubic, hexagonal, trigonal, and tetragonal crystal systems suggests it could become a standard tool for quantum materials simulation.

We extend the symmetry-adapted encoding (SAE) framework to periodic electronic structure, enabling qubit-efficient quantum simulation of crystalline materials. By constructing a $Γ$-point supercell Hamiltonian from a folded $k$-point calculation and systematically identifying all applicable space-group symmetry generators -- including spin-parity, point-group, and crystal translation symmetries -- we obtain qubit Hamiltonians with fewer qubits than the Jordan--Wigner starting point. We benchmark diamond, silicon, 3C-SiC, MgO, NaCl, CsCl, h-BN, wurtzite AlN, $α$-quartz SiO$_2$, and MgF$_2$ using active spaces chosen to preserve complete near-degenerate frontier manifolds across cubic, hexagonal, trigonal, and tetragonal space groups. Across the suite the periodic SAE removes 4--8 qubits. The B2 CsCl benchmark realises eight independent Boolean generators, i.e. a symmetry group isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}_2^8$, reducing CAS(6,7) from 14 to 6 qubits. This exceeds the $\mathbb{Z}_2^5$ maximum of molecular SAE, where only two spin parities and at most three independent Boolean point-group generators are available, because the folded crystal supplies three additional half-translation symmetries. Noiseless UCCSD-VQE benchmarks against exact diagonalisation in the active-space sector show that the reduced encodings preserve the target energies to well below chemical accuracy while reducing variational parameter counts by $3$--$8\times$ and CNOT counts by up to $309\times$. The largest circuit savings occur when translation and point-group generators act independently in the active space, demonstrating that periodic symmetry can be converted directly into both qubit and ansatz compression. The method is implemented in the open-source QuantumSymmetry package and requires no manual specification of symmetry generators.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Symmetries and overparametrization properties of Hamiltonian variational ansatzes for the $(1+1)$d $\mathbb{Z}_2$ lattice gauge theory
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a fundamental challenge in quantum algorithm design: creating variational quantum circuits that are both expressive enough to find good solutions and structured enough to avoid getting trapped in poor local minima. The researchers demonstrate that when variational ansatzes are "overparameterized" - meaning they have more adjustable parameters than strictly necessary - they can escape the barren plateau problem that often plagues quantum optimization algorithms. What makes this work particularly valuable is its focus on gauge theory applications, which are among the most promising near-term use cases for quantum computers in physics simulations, and its rigorous mathematical analysis of how circuit symmetries affect trainability. The finding that optimization speed scales linearly with parameter count provides practical guidance for designing more efficient quantum algorithms for complex many-body systems.

We perform detailed studies of five Hamiltonian variational ansatzes (HVA) based on the Hamiltonian of the $(1+1)$d $\mathbb{Z}_2$ lattice gauge theory. The ansatzes are designed to respect local and global symmetries of the original Hamiltonian and therefore act on a finely segmented state Hilbert space. Following Larocca et al. (2023), we numerically study the dimension of the dynamical Lie algebra (DLA) and the rank of the quantum Fisher information matrix (QFIM) of the ansatzes within specific invariant subspaces. The ansatzes all involve sums of weight-three Paulis in their generators, which is a feature that have so far been underexplored in this context. We also perform numerical experiments to determine the ground state energy of the original Hamiltonian via variational quantum eigensolver (VQE), and observe that overparametrization of the ansatzes coincides with the apparent disappearance of local minima in the loss function, in line with the finding in the reference. Finally, the decay rate of the VQE loss function under gradient descent optimization is revealed to scale linearly with the number of parameters in the ansatz. These results help to enrich the theory of overparameterization of quantum circuits and inform the design of scalable variational ansatzes.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Information-Geometric Bound on the Robustness of Entanglement Generation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research reveals a fundamental connection between quantum Fisher information and the resilience of entanglement generation under real-world noise conditions, providing theorists and experimentalists with a powerful new tool for predicting how robust their quantum systems will be. The work is particularly significant for quantum sensing applications, where entangled states often provide the quantum advantage but are notoriously fragile to parameter fluctuations during generation. By establishing information-geometric bounds on entanglement degradation, this framework could help quantum engineers design more resilient protocols and better understand the fundamental trade-offs between entanglement quality and operational stability. This type of theoretical foundation becomes increasingly crucial as quantum technologies move from proof-of-concept demonstrations toward practical deployment where environmental imperfections are unavoidable.

Entanglement generation is a central resource for quantum information processing, quantum networking, and quantum sensing. In practical implementations, however, entangling interactions are inevitably subject to uncertainty and fluctuations in the interaction strength. We investigate the robustness of entanglement generation in the presence of such imperfections and establish a direct connection between the robustness of entanglement generation and quantum Fisher information (QFI). For two interacting qubits, we show that the reduction in concurrence caused by fluctuations in the interaction parameter is bounded by the QFI with respect to the interaction strength.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: A Class of Multipartite Entangled States Based on State Transitions
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research introduces a fresh perspective on multipartite entanglement by defining quantum states based on "transitions" between adjacent qubits rather than the traditional approaches of global correlations or excitation counts. The key insight that these Transition states can be converted to well-studied Dicke states using standard controlled-X gates creates an intriguing bridge between different mathematical frameworks for understanding many-body entanglement. This work could prove valuable for quantum algorithm designers and experimentalists, as it provides a new toolkit for generating and manipulating complex entangled states that might be more naturally suited to certain quantum computing architectures or applications. While the immediate practical impact remains to be seen, establishing these fundamental connections between different classes of multipartite entangled states strengthens our theoretical foundation for quantum information processing.

We introduce Transition states (T states), denoted by $\ket{T_k^n}$, as a class of multipartite entangled states characterized by a fixed number of state transitions between adjacent qubits. These states form equal-amplitude superpositions over all states with a specified transition count. Unlike Bell states based on two-qubit correlations, GHZ states characterized by global correlations among all qubits, and W and Dicke states based on fixed numbers of qubit excitations, T states are defined by transition counts along an ordered sequence of qubits. We prove that T states are unitarily equivalent to Dicke states through a chain of CX (controlled-X) operations, thereby establishing a direct correspondence between transition-based and excitation-based representations of multipartite entanglement.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quobly Closes €115 Million ($133.5 Million USD) Series A to Industrialize Silicon-Spin Qubit Processors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This funding milestone represents a significant vote of confidence in silicon-spin qubit technology, which promises superior manufacturability compared to more exotic quantum computing approaches by leveraging existing semiconductor fabrication infrastructure. Quobly's substantial Series A round, particularly with backing from France's strategic Deep Tech 2030 initiative and cybersecurity firm SEALSQ, signals Europe's commitment to building domestic quantum capabilities that could challenge the current dominance of superconducting and trapped-ion platforms. The involvement of a post-quantum cryptography specialist as a co-lead investor is particularly telling, suggesting the funding is positioned not just for quantum computing development but for the broader quantum-safe transition that organizations worldwide are beginning to navigate. With silicon-spin qubits potentially offering easier scaling pathways due to their compatibility with conventional chip manufacturing, Quobly's industrialization push could accelerate the timeline for practical quantum advantage in real-world applications.

Grenoble-based hardware venture Quobly has finalized a €115 million ($133.5 million USD) Series A financing round to transition its silicon-based quantum computing architecture from technology validation into active industrial execution. The capital expansion was co-led by Bpifrance (investing via the Deep Tech 2030 fund under the France 2030 initiative), post-quantum cybersecurity specialist SEALSQ, and semiconductor [...] The post Quobly Closes €115 Million ($133.5 Million USD) Series A to Industrialize Silicon-Spin Qubit Processors appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Design Completes Acquisition of Qnami to Expand Nitrogen-Vacancy Diamond Sensing Portfolio
item.description:
Curator's Take: This acquisition signals the growing maturation and commercialization of quantum sensing technologies, particularly nitrogen-vacancy (NV) diamond sensors that can detect magnetic fields with extraordinary precision. Quantum Design's strategic consolidation of specialized quantum sensing companies reflects the increasing market demand for these instruments across applications ranging from medical imaging to materials science research. The deal highlights how established scientific instrumentation companies are positioning themselves to capitalize on the transition of quantum technologies from laboratory curiosities to practical tools, with NV diamond sensors being among the most commercially ready quantum technologies today. This trend of industry consolidation suggests the quantum sensing market is reaching a critical mass where larger players see clear value in acquiring specialized quantum hardware capabilities.

Quantum Design International, Inc. has finalized the acquisition of Basel-based hardware developer Qnami, marking its second corporate consolidation action of 2026. The transaction integrates Qnami's proprietary diamond-based quantum sensing assets into Quantum Design's global scientific instrumentation portfolio. The acquisition follows Quantum Design's January 2026 procurement of the NanoScience division from Oxford Instruments, positioning the parent [...] The post Quantum Design Completes Acquisition of Qnami to Expand Nitrogen-Vacancy Diamond Sensing Portfolio appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Ooredoo Implements Quantum Key Distribution Link on Qatar’s Core Dark Fiber Infrastructure
item.description:
Curator's Take: This deployment marks a significant milestone as Qatar becomes one of the first nations to integrate quantum key distribution directly into its national telecommunications infrastructure, moving QKD beyond laboratory demonstrations into real-world operational use. The collaboration between Ooredoo, academia, and the Ministry of Defense reflects the growing recognition among governments that quantum-safe communications are becoming a national security imperative as quantum computers threaten current encryption standards. What's particularly noteworthy is the integration into existing dark fiber infrastructure, demonstrating how QKD can be practically deployed using current telecom assets rather than requiring entirely new networks. This implementation could serve as a blueprint for other nations looking to quantum-secure their critical communications infrastructure before large-scale quantum computers emerge.

Ooredoo Qatar, in a joint national initiative with Hamad Bin Khalifa University (HBKU) and the Ministry of Defense, has completed the deployment of Qatar’s first quantum-safe communications link. This infrastructure project establishes a fully operational, end-to-end Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) framework integrated directly into a live telecommunications environment. Developed to address the strategic risk of [...] The post Ooredoo Implements Quantum Key Distribution Link on Qatar’s Core Dark Fiber Infrastructure appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Microsoft, Atom Computing, EeroQ update their quantum computing progress
item.description:
Curator's Take: This brief update from Ars Technica highlights how major quantum computing players are maintaining steady momentum with regular progress reports, which signals the industry's growing maturity and commercial viability. While the summary doesn't detail specific breakthroughs, the fact that Microsoft, Atom Computing, and EeroQ are all simultaneously sharing updates suggests coordinated efforts to demonstrate real advancement rather than just research milestones. These regular progress reports are becoming increasingly important as quantum computing transitions from purely academic pursuit to a competitive commercial landscape where companies must prove tangible development to investors and potential enterprise customers. The convergence of updates from both tech giants like Microsoft and specialized quantum startups indicates the field is entering a critical phase where consistent progress reporting becomes as important as the underlying technology itself.

Some quantum computing companies we've covered have done recent progress updates.

Read original at Ars Technica - Science
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Nanomagnets control diamond qubits, pointing to more scalable quantum hardware
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research addresses one of quantum computing's biggest challenges: creating scalable hardware architectures that can maintain qubit quality while growing to practical sizes. Diamond-based qubits, particularly nitrogen-vacancy centers, are already known for their exceptional coherence properties, and the addition of nanomagnetic control represents a potentially game-changing approach to precise qubit manipulation without the bulky infrastructure typically required. The Virginia Commonwealth University team's work could pave the way for more compact, room-temperature quantum processors that are easier to manufacture and deploy outside specialized laboratory environments. While still in early stages, this type of hardware innovation is exactly what's needed to bridge the gap between today's proof-of-concept quantum computers and the large-scale systems that could revolutionize computing across industries.

Quantum computing, once only a theoretical possibility, promises to deliver faster, more energy-efficient computers—but only if scientists can build and scale the hardware needed to run the machines. New research from Virginia Commonwealth University brings scientists one small step closer to quantum computing at a practical scale, which could help dramatically reduce energy usage and computing times in some industries.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Ooredoo, HBKU, Ministry of Defence Launch Qatar’s First Quantum-Safe Network
item.description:
Curator's Take: This deployment marks a significant milestone as Qatar becomes one of the first nations in the Middle East to establish quantum-safe communications infrastructure, positioning itself ahead of the curve for post-quantum cybersecurity. The collaboration between a major telecom provider, academic institution, and defense ministry demonstrates how quantum-resistant technologies are transitioning from research labs to real-world national security applications. This initiative reflects the growing urgency among nations to upgrade their communications infrastructure before large-scale quantum computers potentially render current encryption methods obsolete. Qatar's proactive approach could serve as a model for other countries looking to build quantum-resilient digital infrastructure while there's still time to implement these protections methodically.

Insider Brief Ooredoo Qatar announced the deployment of the first quantum-safe communications link in Qatar, a project the company says establishes a foundation for next-generation cybersecurity and secure digital infrastructure in the country. According to the Qatar Tribune, the initiative was developed in partnership with Hamad Bin Khalifa University (HBKU) and Qatar’s Ministry of Defence. […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Atom Computing Reaches Quantum Error Correction Milestone with Toric Code Demonstration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This demonstration by Atom Computing represents a crucial step toward practical quantum computing, as the company successfully showed that adding more physical qubits actually reduces logical error rates using toric code error correction. This achievement is particularly significant because it validates the theoretical promise of quantum error correction in a real hardware system, moving beyond small-scale proof-of-concept experiments to meaningful error suppression. Atom Computing's neutral-atom platform continues to demonstrate competitive advantages in scaling and connectivity, making this milestone especially noteworthy as the field races toward fault-tolerant quantum computers. The successful implementation of toric codes, which are among the most promising error correction schemes for two-dimensional qubit layouts, could accelerate the timeline for achieving practical quantum advantage in real-world applications.

Logical error rate vs. cycles of error correction in our quantum memory experiment Atom Computing has completed a demonstration of quantum error correction using a toric code configuration on its neutral-atom quantum computing system. The validation metrics indicate that the platform reduces logical error rates when scaling up physical qubit allocations, a characteristic known as [...] The post Atom Computing Reaches Quantum Error Correction Milestone with Toric Code Demonstration appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: High-fidelity neutral atom gates leveraging low-rank Hessian optimization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a breakthrough in making quantum optimal control practically viable by solving one of its biggest experimental hurdles: efficiently calibrating the complex, high-dimensional waveforms needed for high-fidelity gates. The researchers cleverly exploit the mathematical structure of quantum control landscapes to identify just the few critical directions that actually matter for gate performance, dramatically reducing calibration time from what would otherwise be an intractable search through thousands of parameters. Their demonstration of a 99.9% fidelity controlled-Z gate on neutral atom qubits that remains robust under significant laser power variations showcases both the method's effectiveness and its practical resilience. This low-rank Hessian approach could become a standard tool across quantum computing platforms, finally making sophisticated optimal control gates accessible for real quantum algorithms rather than just theoretical demonstrations.

Quantum optimal control can produce fast and robust multi-qubit gates, but experimentally calibrating the resulting high-dimensional waveforms remains challenging because direct searches over large parameter spaces converge slowly. Building on the low-rank structure of quantum-control landscapes, we develop and benchmark a Hessian-based calibration method for optimal-control gates. The method identifies the few waveform directions that affect fidelity to leading order, with the number of directions set by the accessible leakage and coherent error channels, and optimizes only within this principal space using closed-loop experimental feedback. We apply this approach to an amplitude-robust controlled-Z gate on metastable-state 171Yb nuclear-spin qubits. Experimentally, we verify the predicted Hessian-sensitive directions and demonstrate rapid convergence of the optimization protocol.
